headline: Suaram: 6,892 SOSMA Arrests Since 2012; Reported 20–28% Released Without Charge
context: Suaram's 2025 launch reported 6,892 SOSMA arrests since the Act came into force in 2012. Between 20% and 28% of those arrested were reported released without any charge being filed. The 28-day pre-charge detention runs at scale on people never prosecuted.

cards.big: Suaram: 6,892 SOSMA arrests since 2012; reported 20-28% released without any charge filed.
cards.sub: Of arrests under the Security Offences (Special Measures) Act 2012 since enactment, between a fifth and just over a quarter were reported to result in no charge.

cards.big: SOSMA Section 4 allows 28 days of pre-charge police detention since 2012. The Act replaced the ISA but retains extended investigative-detention powers.
cards.sub: The 28-day window is used while authorities decide whether to charge. The 20-28% release-without-charge range shows how often the answer is 'no'.

cards.big: SUHAKAM announced a public inquiry in 2025 into alleged human rights violations linked to a 17 January 2025 Taiping Prison incident that led to a death in custody.
cards.sub: The Taiping inquiry is about prison custody and alleged violations. It is not specifically about SOSMA Section 4 pre-charge detention.

cards.big: Suaram's annual reports are a regularly cited civil-society source on SOSMA usage. Disaggregated official reporting remains limited.
cards.sub: Some aggregate official data appears in parliamentary answers, but disaggregation by arrest, duration, charge and outcome is not consistently published.

cards.big: SOSMA is a routine pre-charge detention regime. Suaram's reported 20-28% release-without-charge rate is the live editorial fact, not a one-time anomaly.
Like a 28-day holding cell where one in four to five occupants is released with no charge, and there is no fully disaggregated public log of who entered or why.
cards.sub: Pre-charge detention used at scale, with limited public accountability for the cases that never produce charges.

cards.big: Genuine SOSMA reform requires disaggregated public reporting on arrests, durations, charges and outcomes, not just renewal debates every five years.
